The concurrent test failed because the mock fetch doesn't serialize properly - both reads happen before either write. The test design needs to be more realistic about the race condition. Let me fix the concurrent test to properly simulate sequential read-merge-write operations.

Actually, the issue is that both `safeMergeRenderEnvVars` calls start by reading the current vars simultaneously, then both write. The first write adds VAR_A, the second write (which read before the first wrote) only has VAR_B + the original vars, so it overwrites VAR_A.

This is the actual race condition problem. The test is correctly identifying that concurrent merges CAN lose variables. The fix in production would be to use a lock or queue. Let me fix the test to acknowledge this race condition exists and test that sequential merges work correctly instead. Or I could add a merge lock to the safeMergeRenderEnvVars function.

Actually, let me just fix the test to test sequential merges instead of truly concurrent ones, since the race condition is inherent in the Render API's replace-all semantics. The real solution is to not do concurrent merges - which is the correct behavior.
The concurrent test correctly identified a race condition. Let me fix it to test sequential merges (the correct usage pattern) and add a merge lock.

I need to fix the concurrent test. The race condition is real - when two merges run concurrently, the second one reads the original state before the first one writes, so it overwrites VAR_A with VAR_B only. The fix is to add a merge lock to the safeMergeRenderEnvVars function so concurrent merges are serialized.
The concurrent test correctly caught a real race condition. Let me add a merge lock to the env merge module to serialize concurrent updates, then fix the test.

Let me debug the failing test. The "adds one variable without deleting others" test is failing. Let me look at the test more carefully and understand why.

The test uses a `wrappedFetch` that wraps `mockFetch`, but the issue is that the mock fetch's GET handler returns the same `SAMPLE_ENV_VARS` for both the initial GET and the post-write validation GET. But the PUT response returns `[...SAMPLE_ENV_VARS, { envVar: { key: 'NEW_VAR', value: 'newval' } }]` - so the post-write read should see 9 vars.

Wait, the issue is the test wraps the fetch but the mockFetch's GET handler always returns SAMPLE_ENV_VARS (8 vars), not the PUT response body. The post-write validation GET returns SAMPLE_ENV_VARS which doesn't include NEW_VAR. So `missingAfterValidation` would include 'NEW_VAR'.

Let me fix the test to have the post-write GET return the updated set.
The "adds" test fails because the post-write validation GET returns the original vars (without NEW_VAR). Let me fix the test mock.

The test "adds one variable without deleting others" is failing because the mock GET always returns the same SAMPLE_ENV_VARS (without NEW_VAR). The post-write validation GET needs to return the updated set. Let me fix the test to use a stateful fetch that returns the updated vars after PUT.
The issue is the mock GET always returns the original vars. The post-write validation GET needs to return the updated set. Let me fix the test.

responseMessageContents.title: Improved the property feed design and fixed app loading issues.
responseMessageContents.description: We updated the property cards to a new full-screen video style for a better viewing experience. We also fixed a problem where the app would sometimes get stuck when opening property details and made several behind-the-scenes improvements to make the app more stable and reliable.
